head_coach.name: Jesse Minter
head_coach.coaching_tree: Macdonald/Martindale → Ravens lineage → Michigan → Chargers. Worked under Don Brown and Mike Macdonald. Shares Wink Martindale coaching DNA but evolved toward zone-heavy, low-blitz, disguise-based defense. Also influenced by time under John Harbaugh in Baltimore (2017-2020).
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Defensive play-caller who builds defenses around four pillars: block destruction, shocking effort, ball disruption, and obnoxious communication. Uses modular play-calling system inherited from Macdonald tree. Prioritizes zone coverage (~80% of snaps), split-safety looks (~50%), minimal blitzing (bottom-5 in blitz rate), and heavy Dime personnel usage. Wants to make the complicated simple for defenders and the simple look complicated for offenses. Stated goal: 'physically dominant team that plays together and plays football the right way.'

offensive_coordinator.name: Declan Doyle
offensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Sean Payton tree (New Orleans 2019-22, Denver 2023-24) → Ben Johnson influence (Chicago Bears OC 2025). Youngest OC in NFL at 29. Mentored by two of the sharpest offensive minds in recent history.
offensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: Began as offensive assistant under Payton in New Orleans, absorbing Saints' timing-based passing concepts and personnel versatility. Moved to Denver as TE coach under Payton, contributing to Bo Nix's 29-TD rookie season. Joined Ben Johnson in Chicago, where Bears went from last in yards to sixth. Johnson's influence adds Shanahan-adjacent concepts: outside zone emphasis, play-action layering, formational multiplicity, making the same thing look different. First-time play-caller in Baltimore — building his own system around Lamar Jackson.
offensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Helped develop Bo Nix into productive rookie QB in Denver. Contributed to Caleb Williams' Year 2 breakout (3,942 yards, 27 TDs). Coached TEs in Denver contributing to top-10 scoring and red zone efficiency. Strong background as TE developer under Payton. Limited track record as primary architect — this is his first play-calling role.

defensive_coordinator.name: Anthony Weaver
defensive_coordinator.coaching_tree: Diverse lineage: Rex Ryan tree (Jets assistant DL), Bills, Browns, Texans DL coach → Ravens DL/run game coordinator/asst HC under Harbaugh (2021-23) → Dolphins DC (2024-25). Former Ravens player (2002-05). DL coaching specialist who expanded to full DC.
defensive_coordinator.scheme_evolution: Started as DL technician. In Baltimore (2021-23), helped build NFL's best run defense and top scoring defense (16.5 ppg in 2023). In Miami, turned middling defense into top-10 unit in Year 1. Adaptable schemer — not married to one system. In Baltimore, Minter will call defensive plays; Weaver manages the front seven, especially the defensive line. His greatest value: developing interior defensive linemen.
defensive_coordinator.development_track_record: Elite DL developer: Nnamdi Madubuike (3rd-round pick → 13-sack All-Pro), Travis Jones, J.J. Watt (comeback 2018), Jadeveon Clowney (breakout), Mario Williams, Kyle Williams, Marcell Dareus. Also developed Jordyn Brooks into first-team All-Pro LB in Miami. Six Pro Bowl defensive linemen coached.

offense.scheme_family: Personnel-driven multiplicity (Payton/Johnson hybrid, Doyle's first original system built around Lamar Jackson)
offense.scheme_detail: Doyle brings a blend of Sean Payton's timing-based passing concepts and Ben Johnson's formational multiplicity and play-action layering. The offense will be built specifically around Jackson's dual-threat ability. Doyle's mantra from Chicago: 'make the same thing look different and different things look the same.' Expect schematic multiplicity — diverse personnel groupings, heavy motion, formation disguise. OL coach Dwayne Ledford brings outside zone run scheme emphasis from Atlanta. However, Minter has stated the offense will be 'personnel driven' and could feature a 'wide variety of run schemes.' Patrick Ricard (fullback) departed, suggesting less 22-personnel heavy sets and a shift toward lighter, more spread-oriented formations than the Harbaugh/Roman era.
offense.run_scheme: Transitioning toward outside zone primary under Ledford, who ran primarily outside zone in Atlanta producing consecutive top-10 rushing outputs. This is a philosophical shift from the Ravens' previous inside zone/gap-heavy approach under Roman/Monken. Baltimore will likely keep some power/gap concepts given Jackson's read-option ability, but the base run scheme is moving outside zone. This demands more athletic, lighter OL who can reach-block and climb to the second level. Derrick Henry remains the bell-cow back.
offense.pass_architecture: Doyle synthesizes Payton's timing routes (slants, digs, crossers on defined timing) with Johnson's play-action layering off the run game. Expect Dagger concepts (deep post + dig), Hi-Cross three-level vertical stretches, and RPO integration given Jackson's processing ability. Play-action rate should be high given the Ravens' established run threat. Doyle emphasized in Chicago tying QB eyes to feet — anticipation throws on defined routes. The TE position will feature prominently in the passing game given Doyle's TE coaching background (Payton's systems always feature TEs). Mark Andrews should be central to the pass design.
offense.personnel_tendencies: Expect diversified groupings: 11 personnel (base spread), 12 personnel (featuring Andrews + another TE), and 21 personnel reduced from prior years with Ricard's departure. Motion usage should increase significantly — both Johnson and Payton are heavy pre-snap motion users. Doyle's Bears offense utilized motion to create defensive conflict and simplify reads for the QB. Shotgun-heavy with Jackson, but under-center work for play-action boots.
offense.motion_tempo: Increased motion expected relative to prior Ravens offenses. Standard to moderate tempo — not a hurry-up attack but not methodical either. Ball-control elements remain given Jackson/Henry's ability to sustain drives on the ground.

defense.scheme_family: Macdonald-tree multiple defense (Minter variant — zone-heavy, disguise-based, Dime-personnel outlier)
defense.scheme_detail: Minter calls defensive plays. This is a modular defensive system where play calls are broken into independent modules — front, pressure, coverage — that can be mixed and matched. Zone coverage on ~80% of snaps. Split-safety looks on ~50% of snaps. Extremely low blitz rate (bottom-5 in NFL) but generates pressure through four-man rush, stunts (top-10 rate), and simulated pressures (top-10 rate). Late rotations and disguise stress QBs pre-snap. The system asks every player to understand the full defensive concept, not just their individual assignment. Minter's defenses are schematic cousins to Macdonald's Seahawks but with more Dime usage and less man coverage.
defense.front_structure: 3-4 base that morphs constantly. Uses four-down nickel fronts as primary sub-package. Massive Dime outlier — used six DBs at third-highest rate in NFL (23.3%). Base defense usage extremely low (~17%). Front four features two interior DL and two EDGE rushers (stand-up OLBs). In LA, used stout edge-setters (Mack) alongside explosive pass rushers (Tuipulotu, Oweh). With Hendrickson signing, expect him as primary pass-rush EDGE with Tavius Robinson/Mike Green as complementary pieces. Weaver's DL expertise will develop the interior.
defense.coverage_philosophy: Zone-dominant. Cover 4 (Quarters), Cover 6 (Quarter-Quarter-Half), Cover 2, and Cover 3 are the primary shells. Split-safety looks create two-high illusions that rotate post-snap. Vision-based zone drops where defenders work to spots but read the QB's intentions rather than matching receivers in pattern-read concepts. Man coverage used sparingly — primarily 2-Man on third downs. Late coverage rotations are a hallmark: show one look pre-snap, rotate to another post-snap. Kyle Hamilton will be deployed like Derwin James was in LA — a hybrid safety playing near the box ~85% of snaps who functions as the defensive chess piece.
defense.pressure_philosophy: Rush four, drop seven. Generate pressure through scheme: stunts (top-10 rate nationally), simulated pressures (top-10), and one-on-one rush matchups created by alignment disguise. Extremely low blitz rate (~21% of snaps). When blitzing, uses safety blitzes (Hamilton/Starks) and delayed LB rushes. Cover 0 used selectively as a tendency-breaker, not a base concept. The philosophy: 'earn the right to rush the passer' by stopping the run first. Edge rushers must be disciplined run defenders who can also win 1v1 pass rush situations.
defense.sub_packages: Dime (6 DBs) is a primary package, not a specialty look. Nickel (5 DBs) is the most common grouping at ~55%. Base (4 DBs) used sparingly (~17%). The 'Big Nickel' or 'Down Safety' (Hamilton-type player who plays near the box like a linebacker) is the key enabler — allows going light in personnel while maintaining run-fit integrity. This creates demand for versatile safeties who can play in the box, cover TEs, and blitz.

QB.scheme_role: The franchise is built around Lamar Jackson, a two-time MVP. The QB in this scheme must operate a diverse run-pass attack featuring heavy play-action, RPOs, designed runs, and timing-based passing concepts. The QB is the run game's most dangerous weapon and must make anticipation throws off play-action while also being a threat to pull and run. Pre-snap processing is essential for RPO execution and reading late-rotating defensive looks.

Arm Strength.reasoning: Doyle's play-action shots require arm talent to push the ball vertically off play fakes, but the system doesn't demand an elite cannon. Jackson's arm is functional, not elite. The scheme creates open windows through design rather than requiring tight-window arm talent.

Accuracy.reasoning: Payton-influenced timing routes (slants, digs, crossers) demand accuracy on defined-timing throws where the ball must arrive as the receiver breaks. Doyle emphasized in Chicago tying QB eyes to feet. Accuracy on intermediate routes is the passing game's engine.

Pocket Presence.reasoning: The scheme creates clean pockets through play-action and boot movement, reducing the need for static pocket navigation. Jackson extends plays with his legs rather than subtle pocket movement. Important but not the defining trait.

Mobility.reasoning: The entire offensive identity is built around the QB's dual-threat ability. Designed runs, read-options, scramble-based play extensions, and boot action all depend on elite mobility. This is THE trait that makes Baltimore's offense different from every other team's.

Decision Making.reasoning: RPO reads, run/pass decisions on option plays, and play-action progressions require fast processing. Doyle's system adds formational complexity — the QB must quickly identify defensive structure and make the right choice pre-snap and at the mesh point.

Leadership.reasoning: Jackson is the established leader. For a backup, leadership matters situationally but is secondary to system fit. The scheme doesn't demand a vocal field general — it demands a dynamic athlete.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: RPO execution requires reading the conflict defender pre-snap. Doyle's multiple formation looks give the QB pre-snap indicators. Important for maximizing the scheme but the design simplifies reads for the QB.

QB.ideal_archetype: The ideal QB is Lamar Jackson himself — a dynamic dual-threat who can execute timing routes off play-action while being the most dangerous runner on the field. The backup must approximate this skill set: a mobile QB who can run the read-option and boot game, not a statue. Think a younger Jackson, Josh Allen's mobility profile, or Jayden Daniels' blend of run threat and passing accuracy. A pure pocket passer like Kirk Cousins would be a terrible fit because the run game architecture depends on the QB run threat.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Designed QB runs, read-options, and boot action all require legitimate speed to threaten the edge and force defensive conflict. A slow QB removes an entire dimension.

height.reasoning: No strong scheme-driven height preference. Jackson is 6-2. The system doesn't require a tall QB to see over the line — movement and boot concepts create throwing lanes.

weight.reasoning: Must be durable enough to absorb contact on designed runs while maintaining elusiveness. Too heavy sacrifices mobility; too light risks durability.

QB.development_context: Doyle is a first-time play-caller building his system around Jackson. The offensive scheme will be tailored to the QB, not the reverse. For a backup or developmental prospect, Doyle's Payton/Johnson background suggests he can develop accuracy and processing through schematic structure. What cannot be developed: elite mobility. A prospect's running ability must arrive Day 1.
QB.scheme_fit_narrative: This offense is a QB-run-threat-first system. Everything branches from the threat of the QB pulling and running — the play-action, the boots, the RPOs, the run game itself. Without a mobile QB, the offense becomes generic and loses its schematic advantage. We need a runner who can throw, not a thrower who can run.

RB.scheme_role: The primary ball carrier in an outside zone-based run scheme who must also handle inside zone, gap concepts, and read-option mesh plays. With Derrick Henry entrenched, the RB operates behind a mobile offensive line with a dual-threat QB. The RB must press the hole on outside zone, then cut backside when the frontside is walled off. Pass catching is secondary but will increase under Doyle's Payton influence. Pass protection matters — Jackson's escapability reduces the burden but the RB must be willing.

Vision.reasoning: Outside zone demands elite vision to read blocks developing horizontally, identify cutback lanes, and make one decisive cut. The shift from gap/power schemes to Ledford's OZ system makes vision THE differentiating trait. The RB must process reach blocks in real-time.

Contact Balance.reasoning: Ledford's outside zone creates horizontal running where the back takes contact while cutting back. Must stay on feet through arm tackles and maintain yardage after initial contact. Henry excels here.

Power.reasoning: Still important for short-yardage and goal-line situations, but less critical than under the previous gap-heavy scheme. Outside zone rewards lateral agility over straight-line power.

Elusiveness.reasoning: Outside zone creates one-on-one matchups in space after the cutback. Elusiveness to make the first defender miss extends runs from 4-yard gains to 12-yard gains. Ledford's Atlanta backs (Bijan Robinson) thrived on elusiveness in space.

Speed.reasoning: Outside zone is a horizontal run scheme that rewards backs who can press the edge and burst through cutback lanes. Speed creates home-run potential that the scheme is designed to generate. Ledford's Falcons consistently produced explosive run plays.

Pass Catching.reasoning: Doyle's Payton influence increases screen and check-down usage relative to prior Ravens offenses. The RB will be asked to catch swing passes, screens, and check-downs more frequently. Not a primary demand but a meaningful upgrade in importance.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Jackson's mobility reduces the need for RB pass protection, but blitz pickup ability is still necessary on drop-back plays. Not a scheme-critical trait but baseline competence required.

RB.ideal_archetype: The ideal RB is a one-cut zone runner with burst and home-run speed who can press the hole, set up blocks, and explode through cutback lanes. Think Bijan Robinson — Ledford's star back in Atlanta who mastered outside zone with vision, speed, and elusiveness. Or Jahmyr Gibbs, who thrived in Johnson's outside-zone-heavy Lions attack. The Ravens don't need a between-the-tackles bruiser anymore — they need a back who can make the most of Ledford's lateral run designs. Henry is the power complement; the developmental back should be the speed/elusiveness piece.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Outside zone rewards speed to press the edge and create explosive plays through cutback lanes. A slow back negates the scheme's horizontal advantage.

weight.reasoning: Must be durable enough to handle 15+ carries but light enough to maintain burst on zone runs. Too heavy sacrifices the lateral agility OZ demands.

3-cone drill.reasoning: Zone running demands change of direction to execute cutbacks. The 3-cone measures the lateral agility and cutting ability essential to OZ success.

RB.development_context: Ledford developed Bijan Robinson into one of the NFL's most productive zone runners. His OL coaching creates the blocking environment; the RB must arrive with vision and burst. Vision is extremely difficult to develop — it's largely innate. Speed and elusiveness are also physical traits that must arrive. Pass catching and pass protection can be coached up.
RB.scheme_fit_narrative: We're shifting to an outside zone run scheme that rewards backs who can press the line of scrimmage, read blocks developing in front of them, and explode through cutback lanes. The Henry-type bruiser is the complement, not the prototype. We want a fast, decisive runner who makes one cut and goes. If a prospect can't read zone blocking, all the speed in the world won't help.

WR.scheme_role: The WR room must support a dual-threat QB and a run-first identity while creating explosive plays off play-action. The WR1 runs the full route tree on timing concepts (slants, digs, posts). The WR2/3 must win contested catches and create separation on designed play-action shots. All WRs must block in the run game — Baltimore's run-first identity demands WR buy-in. Doyle's Payton/Johnson influence means WRs will be asked to run diverse route concepts from multiple alignments and be interchangeable formationally.

Route Running.reasoning: Doyle's Payton-influenced timing passing demands precise route breaks and depth integrity. Doyle reminded receivers in Chicago of 'the necessity of route depth integrity.' Routes must be run to exact depths to allow the QB to throw on time.

Separation.reasoning: Zone-heavy defenses the Ravens face in-division (Steelers, Bengals) require WRs to create separation against zone drops. The timing system doesn't create massive pre-snap mismatches — the WR must win at the top of the route.

Hands.reasoning: Jackson's accuracy can be inconsistent, and play-action passes arrive with velocity. Reliable hands matter, but the scheme creates more open looks than contested-catch situations.

YAC Ability.reasoning: Doyle's Johnson-influenced concepts (shallow crosses, quick screens, RPO throws) are designed to get the ball to receivers in space. YAC turns 5-yard completions into 15-yard gains. This was a hallmark of the Lions offense Doyle helped build in Chicago.

Speed.reasoning: Play-action shots off the run game are the explosive play generator. Deep speed stretches the defense vertically to open up the run game. The run-threat identity depends on defenses respecting the deep ball.

Contested Catches.reasoning: Jackson's play style creates some off-platform throws into tight windows. A WR who can win 50/50 balls adds value, but the scheme is designed to create separation rather than force contested targets.

Release Package.reasoning: Baltimore's AFC North schedule features physical corners (Steelers, Browns). Release skill at the line matters against press coverage, but the scheme uses motion and formation to create free releases.

WR.ideal_archetype: The ideal WR is a precise route runner with YAC ability and enough speed to threaten deep off play-action. Think Amon-Ra St. Brown — Johnson's best WR in Detroit who combined route-running precision with elite YAC and reliable hands. Or DJ Moore, who thrived under Johnson/Doyle in Chicago. Not a pure deep threat and not a possession-only receiver — a versatile weapon who can win at all three levels and contribute as a blocker in the run game.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Must threaten vertically off play-action to keep defenses honest. Pure possession receivers without speed limit the offense's explosive play potential.

height.reasoning: No strong height preference. The scheme values route runners and YAC players over size. Zay Flowers (5-10) has been productive in Baltimore.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to block on the perimeter in the run game. Slight receivers who can't sustain blocks hurt the run scheme.

WR.development_context: Doyle's background is as a TE coach, not a WR coach. New WR coach Zach Colbert comes from Denver where Courtland Sutton eclipsed 1,000 yards but the Broncos had the second-most dropped passes in the league. Route running technique can be developed. Speed and hands are harder to coach. Expect this staff to prioritize WR athleticism and develop the technical aspects.
WR.scheme_fit_narrative: We need receivers who can run precise routes at defined depths, turn short catches into long gains, and block their tails off on run plays. The play-action passing game will create opportunities, but the receiver has to finish those opportunities. A diva WR who won't block is useless here. We want hungry, athletic route runners who bring toughness to the run game.

TE.scheme_role: Central to the offense. Doyle's entire coaching career has been built around TEs — he was Payton's TE coach in Denver and helped feature TEs in both the passing and run game. The TE1 (Mark Andrews) functions as a primary receiving target from multiple alignments — in-line, slot, flexed out. The TE2 must be a plus blocker who can also contribute in the passing game. TEs will be featured in red zone concepts, 12-personnel formations, and play-action designs. With Isaiah Likely and Charlie Kolar departing, the Ravens need TE depth badly.

Receiving.reasoning: Doyle's Payton-tree systems have always featured TEs as primary pass catchers. In Denver, Doyle's TE group contributed to a top-10 scoring offense. In New Orleans, Payton used Jimmy Graham and Jared Cook as centerpiece receivers. The TE must be a reliable receiving weapon.

Route Running.reasoning: The TE will be asked to run a diverse route tree from in-line, slot, and flexed alignments. Seam routes, digs, corner routes, and shallow crosses are staples. Doyle's emphasis on route depth integrity applies to TEs as well.

Blocking.reasoning: Ledford's outside zone scheme requires the TE to execute reach blocks and seal the edge. The run-first identity demands blocking competence. With Kolar (the blocking TE) departing for the Chargers, Baltimore needs blocking from the TE room.

Athleticism.reasoning: The TE must flex between in-line blocking and route running from the slot. Minter's personnel-light defensive philosophy suggests the Ravens will often be in 12-personnel on offense to force defenses to stay heavier, creating mismatches when the TE goes to the slot.

Hands.reasoning: The TE will see significant targets in this system, including red zone looks. Reliable hands are necessary. Andrews has been the franchise's all-time receiving leader at the position.

Speed.reasoning: Seam-stretch ability matters for play-action concepts, but the TE doesn't need WR-level speed. Must be fast enough to threaten the seam and separate against linebackers.

TE.ideal_archetype: The ideal TE is a versatile player who can line up in-line and block on outside zone runs, then flex to the slot and run the full route tree. Think T.J. Hockenson (whom Doyle's 2024 Vikings coaching peers helped produce a 95-catch season) or Dallas Goedert — reliable receivers who also contribute as inline blockers. Not a pure receiving weapon (that's Andrews' role) and not a pure blocker (that role left with Kolar). A complete TE.

height.reasoning: Red zone target and seam-stretch weapon. Height creates contested catch ability and mismatch potential against linebackers and safeties.

weight.reasoning: Must be able to block as an in-line TE on outside zone runs. Too light to sustain blocks against NFL EDGEs and DEs.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Must threaten the seam on play-action. A slow TE gets covered easily by linebackers in zone and loses seam-stretch value.

TE.development_context: Doyle is an elite TE developer. His entire coaching career started as a TE coach under Payton. He can develop receiving skills and route technique. Blocking can also be developed at the TE position under Ledford's tutelage. This staff can take a raw athletic TE and develop both receiving and blocking skills — a rare developmental advantage. A prospect with plus athleticism and raw skills is a strong fit.
TE.scheme_fit_narrative: This offense will feature the TE position more than almost any team in the league. Doyle built his career coaching TEs, and Payton's offenses have always used TEs as primary weapons. We need a complete TE who can block on zone runs and catch 50+ balls. Raw athleticism with coachable technique is ideal — this staff will develop the rest.

OT.scheme_role: The OT in this scheme must execute outside zone reach blocks, climbing to the second level to seal linebackers. The shift from gap/power blocking to Ledford's outside zone system demands athletic OTs who can move laterally. Pass protection remains critical for Jackson's play-action passing, but the boot game reduces pure dropback pass protection demands. The LT must handle speed rushers; the RT must handle power from EDGE defenders in the AFC North.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Jackson's mobility reduces static pass protection demand, but he still drops back on timing routes. AFC North features elite pass rushers (Hendrickson formerly, Garrett, Watt). Must be able to anchor and mirror.

Run Blocking.reasoning: Ledford's outside zone is THE scheme identity. OTs must execute reach blocks, sustain lateral movement, and climb to the second level to block linebackers. Run blocking is the primary demand in this run-first offense.

Footwork.reasoning: Outside zone reach blocks require explosive lateral first steps and the footwork to seal defenders on the backside. Ledford's system demands OTs who can get lateral displacement immediately at the snap.

Anchor.reasoning: Important against power rushers in the AFC North, but the boot game and Jackson's mobility reduce the number of static anchor situations. Secondary trait.

Athleticism.reasoning: DeCosta explicitly noted the Ravens may target 'lighter, more athletic and agile' OL prospects under Ledford. Athleticism to reach-block, pull on counters, and work to the second level is scheme-critical.

Strength.reasoning: Raw strength matters less than in a gap/power scheme. Outside zone rewards movement skills over brute force. Adequate strength is needed but it's not the differentiator.

OT.ideal_archetype: The ideal OT is an athletic mover who can execute reach blocks on outside zone, climb to the second level, and sustain blocks in space. Think Jake Matthews or Chris Lindstrom's tackle counterparts in Atlanta — the type of OTs Ledford coached to consecutive top-10 rushing outputs. Not a mauler who dominates in phone-booth run blocking — an athlete who can work laterally and pass-set on the move. Penei Sewell's combination of athleticism and technique is the ceiling.

short shuttle.reasoning: Ledford's outside zone requires OTs to get lateral displacement on the first step and reach the backside. Short shuttle measures the lateral agility critical to this scheme.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Must be able to climb to the second level and block linebackers in space on zone runs. A slow OT can't execute this scheme's run blocking demands.

weight.reasoning: DeCosta signaled the Ravens may move away from massive, immobile OL. Lighter frames maintain the athleticism needed for Ledford's zone scheme.

OT.development_context: Ledford is widely regarded as one of the best OL developers in the NFL. He was specifically hired for his run-game coaching expertise. He can develop run-blocking technique in zone schemes. Pass protection refinement is also coachable. This staff can take an athletic OT and teach zone-blocking footwork. What must arrive: baseline athleticism and movement skills.
OT.scheme_fit_narrative: We're looking for athletes, not anchors. The shift to outside zone means we need OTs who can move. A big, stiff tackle who can pass-set in a phone booth won't work in Ledford's run game. Give us a mover who can reach-block, climb to linebackers, and sustain blocks in space. We'll teach the technique.

IOL.scheme_role: The interior OL is undergoing a complete overhaul. Tyler Linderbaum (center) departed to the Raiders. John Simpson was re-signed at guard. Emery Jones Jr. projects at the other guard. The IOL must execute outside zone reach blocks, create lateral displacement, and handle combo blocks to the second level. The center must handle the snap-and-reach mechanics of OZ while making line calls. Guards must pull on counter concepts and sustain blocks in space.

Pass Protection.reasoning: Jackson's mobility reduces interior pressure situations, but the timing passing game requires a clean pocket. Must handle NFL DTs in pass protection, though boot action reduces static reps.

Run Blocking.reasoning: OZ run blocking is the IOL's primary responsibility. Must execute reach blocks, combo blocks, and climb to the second level. Linderbaum was described as 'a perfect fit for Ledford's outside-zone run schemes.' The replacement must have similar movement skills.

Pulling.reasoning: Counter and power concepts supplement the OZ base. Guards must be able to pull and lead through the hole. Johnson's Lions offense used pulling guards extensively, and Doyle brings that influence.

Strength.reasoning: Must sustain blocks against NFL nose tackles and 3-techniques. Important for combo blocks where the IOL must initially double-team then release to the second level.

Anchor.reasoning: Interior pass rush from DTs requires adequate anchor ability. Less critical than in a pure dropback offense because Jackson moves the pocket.

Versatility.reasoning: With the entire interior in flux, the Ravens need IOL who can play multiple positions. Center-guard versatility is especially valuable. Simpson has played guard, and Jones was a tackle at LSU playing guard in the NFL. Positional flexibility is a premium.

IOL.ideal_archetype: The ideal IOL is an athletic interior player who can move in space — think Tyler Linderbaum's movement profile. The scheme demands quick-footed guards and centers who can reach-block, pull, and get to the second level. Not a 330-pound road grader — a 305-315 pound athlete with lateral quickness. Zack Martin's technical prowess combined with Jason Kelce's movement ability is the archetype. At guard, Chris Lindstrom in Atlanta — a player Ledford coached — is the template.

short shuttle.reasoning: Outside zone demands lateral agility from interior linemen to execute reach blocks. The short shuttle is the single most predictive measurable for zone-scheme IOL success.

weight.reasoning: Lighter, more athletic IOL profiles fit the zone scheme better. DeCosta signaled a shift toward lighter, more athletic OL.

bench press.reasoning: Adequate upper body strength for sustaining blocks, but no hard floor. The scheme values movement over brute force.

IOL.development_context: Ledford is the primary IOL developer. He produced elite run-blocking units in Atlanta. He can develop zone-blocking technique from raw athletes. The loss of Linderbaum creates a critical need — the Ravens need a center prospect who can step in and execute Ledford's OZ scheme. Versatility is coachable to some degree but natural position flexibility is preferred.
IOL.scheme_fit_narrative: We lost our best interior lineman and we're changing the run scheme. We need quick, athletic interior linemen who can reach-block and pull. The days of giant immobile guards are over. Ledford wants movers. If a prospect can't change direction quickly, he can't play in this zone scheme no matter how strong he is.

EDGE.scheme_role: The EDGE in Minter's defense is a stand-up OLB who must primarily win 1v1 pass rush situations in a four-man rush. With the lowest blitz rate in the NFL, EDGE rushers must generate pressure without extra help. But Minter's philosophy demands earning the right to rush the passer — meaning run defense and edge-setting come first. The EDGE must be disciplined in run fits with light-box personnel, as the defense often plays with minus-box numbers. Must be able to drop into shallow zones on simulated pressures and fire zones. Hendrickson (just signed, $112M) is the premier pass rusher; Green, Robinson, and draft picks provide depth.

Pass Rush.reasoning: With the third-lowest blitz rate in the NFL, four-man pressure must generate sacks. Minter's EDGE rushers must win 1v1. Tuipulotu had 13 sacks, Oweh 7.5, and Mack 5.5 in Minter's scheme. The system creates advantageous matchups through alignment, but the EDGE must finish.

Bend.reasoning: Minter's stand-up EDGE rushers win around the corner. The scheme uses stunts (top-10 rate) that create edges to bend around. Tuipulotu and Oweh both win with bend and flexibility rather than pure power.

First Step.reasoning: The four-man rush relies on winning the timing battle. An explosive first step generates immediate pressure that forces the QB to make quick decisions — feeding directly into the zone coverage behind. Minter simplified things for Oweh by allowing him 'more freedom as a pass rusher.'

Power.reasoning: Power matters for bull-rush counters and run-game edge setting, but Minter's scheme prioritizes speed-to-power conversion over pure bull rush. The scheme creates 1v1 matchups where finesse wins more often than brute force.

Motor.reasoning: Minter's four pillars include 'shocking effort.' The EDGE must pursue from behind, chase plays across the field, and never give up on the rush. Effort-based rush production is a hallmark — not every pressure is a clean 1v1 win.

Run Defense.reasoning: This defense plays with light boxes and minus-front personnel. The EDGE must set the edge in the run game because there are fewer bodies to compensate. Mack's ability to collapse and seal running lanes was critical to Minter's scheme in LA. 'You have to earn the right to rush the passer.'

EDGE.ideal_archetype: The ideal EDGE is a versatile stand-up OLB who wins with a combination of first-step explosion and bend, can set the edge against the run with discipline, and has the motor to chase plays down from behind. Tuli Tuipulotu is the prototype — a second-round pick who broke out under Minter with 13 sacks using explosive athleticism and effort rather than elite technique or power. Odafe Oweh's post-trade transformation under Minter is another example: simplified responsibilities, more freedom, explosive results. Not a pure power rusher and not a speed-only rusher — a complete, effort-based defender.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Speed to the edge is critical for four-man rush production. Minter's EDGE rushers (Tuipulotu, Oweh) are all athletic, fast-twitch defenders who win with explosion.

weight.reasoning: Stand-up OLB in a 3-4. Must be light enough to drop into coverage on simulated pressures but heavy enough to hold the edge against the run.

arm length.reasoning: Length helps with hand usage on pass rush and setting the edge. Not a hard requirement but a plus trait in this scheme.

3-cone drill.reasoning: Must be able to change direction on stunts and simulated pressures. The modular system sometimes asks EDGE players to redirect mid-rush.

EDGE.development_context: Minter is an elite EDGE developer. He transformed Oweh from a struggling Raven (2.5 sacks in half a season) to a dominant Charger (7.5 sacks in 12 games) by simplifying his role and giving him pass-rush freedom. He developed Tuipulotu from a second-round pick into a Pro Bowler. Green (2025 rookie) showed flashes but needs refinement. This staff can develop raw athletic pass rushers into productive players. What must arrive: explosion, motor, and baseline bend.
EDGE.scheme_fit_narrative: We don't need a one-trick pony. Our EDGE rusher has to set the edge, rush the passer with a four-man front, and occasionally drop into coverage on simulated pressures. We need an explosive athlete with a relentless motor who can be coached into a complete defender. This staff turned Oweh and Tuipulotu into stars — we're looking for the next version of that.

DL.scheme_role: Interior DL in Minter's defense anchor a light-personnel front. With frequent Dime looks (6 DBs) and a four-man front, the DL must command double teams and control gaps. The NT/DT must handle run responsibilities with minimal linebacker support behind them (light boxes). Pass rush penetration is valued but gap integrity comes first. Weaver's DL coaching expertise is the key developmental engine here. Nnamdi Madubuike's future is uncertain (season-ending neck injury in Week 2). Travis Jones is the anchor, and Aeneas Peebles is a developmental interior rusher.

Pass Rush.reasoning: Interior pressure is critical in a four-man rush system. With EDGE rushers winning on the outside, interior disruption closes the pocket. Madubuike's 13-sack season in 2023 under Weaver's coaching showed what interior rush can do.

Run Defense.reasoning: With light boxes and Dime personnel, the DL must eat blocks and control gaps without linebacker support. The scheme accepts some run-game inefficiency (bait-and-switch philosophy) but the DL must prevent gashing runs.

First Step.reasoning: Interior penetration creates disruption, but gap discipline matters more than pure penetration in this scheme. A DT who shoots gaps and gets washed out of his lane hurts the light-box run defense.

Hand Usage.reasoning: Must defeat blocks with hand technique to maintain gap control. Weaver's DL coaching is heavily technique-based — he developed Madubuike's hand usage from raw third-round talent to elite production.

Motor.reasoning: Effort pillar applies to DL as well. Must play through double teams and pursue laterally. Important but secondary to gap discipline and technique.

Strength.reasoning: Must hold the point of attack against double teams with minimal help. The light-box scheme means the DL takes on combo blocks without linebacker cleanup. Base strength to anchor is critical.

DL.ideal_archetype: The ideal interior DL is a two-gap anchor who can hold the point of attack, control his gaps, and generate interior pressure through technique and hand usage. Nnamdi Madubuike is the prototype — a versatile 3-technique who stuffs the run and rushes the passer with equal effectiveness. For the nose, a space-eating anchor like Travis Jones who commands attention and frees up the EDGE rushers. Not a pure penetrator (that player gets washed in a light-box scheme) and not a pure space-eater (must contribute to the pass rush).

weight.reasoning: Must be heavy enough to anchor against double teams in a light-box scheme. Too light and OL drive the DL off the ball.

bench press.reasoning: Upper body strength to hold the point and defeat blocks. Weaver develops hand usage, but raw strength must be present.

3-cone drill.reasoning: Interior agility matters for stunt execution (top-10 stunt rate). Must be able to redirect on twists and games.

DL.development_context: Weaver is an elite interior DL developer — six Pro Bowl DL coached. He turned Madubuike from a raw third-round pick into a 13-sack All-Pro. He developed J.J. Watt's comeback, Clowney's breakout, and Travis Jones. This is the strongest DL development environment in the NFL. Raw athletes with physical traits will be developed into technique-refined players. What must arrive: baseline strength and motor. Everything else can be taught.
DL.scheme_fit_narrative: Weaver is the best DL developer in football. Give us a big, strong, twitchy athlete and he'll turn them into a productive player. The scheme needs DL who can hold the point and generate push — we can't have penetrators who blow their gap in a light-box defense. We want an athlete who can be coached.

LB.scheme_role: Minter's defense uses minimal LB personnel — often only one or two LBs on the field with Dime and heavy Nickel packages. The MIKE LB (Roquan Smith) is the every-down defender who must play 85%+ of snaps. The second LB rotates based on package. The LB must process quickly in zone coverage, fit the run with light-box support, and be versatile enough to handle both. Minter's Chargers used LBs like Daiyan Henley as QB spies and box-fill players who also dropped into zone. The LB room is thin after Smith — Trenton Simpson is the primary backup.

Tackling.reasoning: With light boxes and fewer defenders near the ball, every tackle attempt must be efficient. Missed tackles in a light-box scheme create explosive runs. Minter's defenses accept some run-game inefficiency but cannot afford whiffs.

Coverage.reasoning: LBs drop into zone on the vast majority of defensive snaps (~80% zone). Must handle hook/curl zones, read QB eyes, and break on the ball. Vision-based zone drops require processing ability. This is the defining trait for an off-ball LB in Minter's system.

Pass Rush.reasoning: LBs rarely blitz in this scheme (bottom-5 blitz rate). Delayed rushes and QB spy responsibilities exist but are supplementary. Not a primary demand.

Instincts.reasoning: The modular system asks LBs to understand the full defensive concept, not just their individual assignment. Must process offensive formations, motion, and route combinations to be in position.

Athleticism.reasoning: Must cover ground in zone drops, close on the ball in space, and match up against TEs and RBs in man on occasion. The light-personnel scheme demands athletic LBs who can function almost like safeties.

Range.reasoning: Sideline-to-sideline range helps compensate for light boxes. Must be able to chase plays from the middle of the field to the boundary.

Block Shedding.reasoning: Must shed blocks to make plays in the run game, but the scheme's light-box philosophy means OL often climb to LBs. Must be able to avoid and defeat second-level blocks.

Pre-Snap Diagnosis.reasoning: The modular system requires understanding formation, personnel, and likely offensive concept. Helps with run-pass key reads and zone drop alignment.

LB.ideal_archetype: The ideal LB is an athletic, coverage-first defender who can process zone concepts, make tackles in space, and hold up as an every-down player. Roquan Smith is the prototype — an elite three-down LB who tackles efficiently, covers zone, and processes at an elite level. The backup/developmental LB should resemble Daiyan Henley in LA — an athletic LB who can spy the QB, drop into zone, and fit the run despite not being the biggest player. Not a downhill thumper who can't cover — coverage is king in this defense.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Must be able to cover ground in zone drops and close on receivers. The light-personnel scheme needs LBs who can function almost as safeties at times.

weight.reasoning: Light enough to cover but heavy enough to shed blocks and make tackles. The scheme doesn't need a 260-pound run-stuffer — it needs an athlete.

3-cone drill.reasoning: Change of direction is critical for zone drops — must redirect from read key to receiver.

LB.development_context: Weaver developed Jordyn Brooks into a first-team All-Pro in Miami. The Ravens have experience developing athletic LBs (Patrick Queen, Trenton Simpson). Coverage technique and zone recognition can be developed. What must arrive: athleticism, instincts, and tackling ability. A raw athlete with coverage potential is a strong developmental fit.
LB.scheme_fit_narrative: We only keep one or two LBs on the field most plays. The ones who stay must be able to cover — zone drops, spy duties, matching RBs and TEs. If a linebacker can't cover, he's off the field in this defense. We want athletes who can process and play in space, not box-stuffers who are liabilities in the pass game.

CB.scheme_role: CBs in Minter's defense play primarily zone coverage (~80% of snaps). Split-safety looks mean CBs often play with a deep safety on their side, reducing solo man responsibilities. The CBs must be able to play vision-based zone drops — reading the QB's eyes and breaking on the ball rather than matching receivers. However, 2-Man on third downs requires man-coverage ability. Minter used late-day draft picks (Tarheeb Still, Cam Hart — 5th rounders) who became starters in his zone scheme, suggesting the system elevates CBs. Marlon Humphrey and Nate Wiggins are the top two; Chidobe Awuzie was re-signed as CB3.

Man Coverage.reasoning: 2-Man is used on third downs, and isolated man coverage exists situationally. Not the primary demand but must be competent. The scheme uses zone ~80% of the time.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Minter's zone coverage creates opportunities for CBs to read the QB and break on the ball. The Chargers had 10 interceptions in their last five games of 2025. Ball disruption is one of Minter's four defensive pillars. CBs must finish takeaway opportunities.

Zone Coverage.reasoning: Zone coverage on ~80% of snaps makes this THE defining trait. CBs must understand zone concepts, read QB eyes, maintain proper depth, and break on the ball. Vision-based drops require processing — not just sticking to a spot but reading route combinations.

Speed.reasoning: Must be able to recover when beaten in zone and close on receivers once the ball is thrown. Speed enables the aggressive ball-hawking breaks that generate turnovers.

Press.reasoning: Press coverage is not a primary tool in this zone-heavy scheme. Used situationally to disrupt timing. Not a scheme-critical trait.

Nickel.reasoning: Dime usage at the third-highest rate in NFL means CBs must be able to work inside and outside. The nickel role is critical — CBs who can play multiple spots provide the versatility this defense demands.

CB.ideal_archetype: The ideal CB is a zone technician with elite ball skills who can read the QB, break on the ball, and generate turnovers. Not a press-man shutdown artist — a zone hawk who plays with eyes in the backfield and creates takeaways. Tarheeb Still and Cam Hart (5th-round picks who became starters under Minter) are the developmental examples. For a starter, think Budda Baker-era Patrick Peterson in Arizona — a zone-savvy CB who generated turnovers through anticipation rather than pure physical dominance. Or Marlon Humphrey at his best — a versatile CB who can play outside zone and slot with ball skills.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Recovery speed to close on receivers after reading the QB. Zone CBs must be fast enough to break on the ball from depth.

height.reasoning: No extreme height requirement. The scheme values movement and processing over size. Both Still and Hart are average-sized corners.

weight.reasoning: Must be physical enough to tackle in run support (light boxes mean CBs are asked to support the run) but not so heavy that it limits agility in zone.

CB.development_context: Minter and new DB coach Mike Mickens (from Notre Dame) have elite CB development track records. Minter turned 5th-round picks into starters in one year. The zone scheme simplifies responsibilities for young CBs — they don't need to man-up 1v1 against NFL receivers immediately. Vision and processing can be taught. What must arrive: speed, ball skills, and football intelligence. This is an ideal landing spot for a raw but athletic CB prospect.
CB.scheme_fit_narrative: We don't need a lockdown man-cover corner. We need a smart, fast, ball-hawking zone defender who reads the QB and makes plays on the ball. This scheme puts CBs in position to make turnovers if they have the instincts and ball skills. A raw athlete with speed and football IQ is ideal — we turned 5th-rounders into starters.

S.scheme_role: The safety position is the most critical defensive position in Minter's scheme. Kyle Hamilton is the defensive centerpiece — a hybrid safety who plays near the box on ~85% of snaps, functioning as a quasi-linebacker, pass rusher, and coverage player. The second safety (Malaki Starks, recently drafted) plays the deep-field role in split-safety looks. Minter's modular system asks safeties to rotate between pre-snap and post-snap responsibilities — showing one alignment then moving to another. The 'Big Nickel' safety is the key enabler that allows Dime personnel to function. Alohi Gilman departed in free agency; depth is a need.

Man Coverage.reasoning: Safeties must cover TEs in man situations (2-Man on third downs). Hamilton is asked to match up on TEs regularly. Important situationally but zone is primary.

Range.reasoning: Split-safety looks require deep-field coverage range. The deep safety must cover half the field in Cover 2/Cover 4/Cover 6 looks. Range is critical for preventing explosive plays — Minter's defenses accept short gains to prevent the big play.

Ball Skills.reasoning: Turnovers are a defensive pillar. Safeties in split-safety zone looks get opportunities to read the QB and intercept. The Chargers generated 10+ interceptions in the second half of 2025, many from safety play.

Tackling.reasoning: The 'Big Nickel' safety must tackle in the box like a linebacker. With light personnel, every missed tackle at safety leads to explosive plays. Hamilton's tackling near the line of scrimmage is essential to the scheme's light-box viability.

Speed.reasoning: Must cover ground in deep-half zones and close on receivers. The scheme's late rotations require safeties to move quickly from pre-snap alignment to post-snap responsibility.

Nickel.reasoning: Minter's Dime personnel asks safeties to function as slot defenders, box players, and blitzers. Derwin James was used at nickel, dime, safety, and box positions. The scheme demands an extremely versatile safety who can play multiple roles within a single play. This is the defining characteristic of Minter's safety usage.

S.ideal_archetype: The ideal safety is Kyle Hamilton or Derwin James — a true hybrid who can line up at every level of the defense. Near the box as a run-fitter, in the slot as a nickel, over the top as a deep safety, and as a blitzer off the edge. Minter explicitly said of James: 'The closer he is to the action, the better he is.' He then noted James 'gives me the flexibility of what are the best pieces around him.' For the deep safety, think Elijah Molden — a smart, instinctive player who makes zone rotations work. The safety room MUST have at least one Hamilton/James-type chess piece.

40-yard dash.reasoning: Must cover deep halves and close on receivers from depth. The split-safety scheme demands range athletes.

weight.reasoning: The Big Nickel safety must tackle in the box. Too light to hold up as a box defender in run support. Must be physical enough to function as a quasi-linebacker.

height.reasoning: The Hamilton/James archetype is a large safety (Hamilton 6-4, James 6-2) who can match up on TEs and function as a box player. Size is a plus trait for this scheme's versatility demands.

vertical jump.reasoning: Ball skills in the air are critical for interceptions in zone. Must be able to high-point the ball on deep shots.

S.development_context: Minter revived Derwin James' career (from worst season to All-Pro form). He developed Elijah Molden from a role player to a key piece. The scheme develops safeties by putting them in positions to succeed — zone coverage simplifies responsibilities while versatility is added gradually. New safeties coach P.J. Volker comes from Navy, where he developed defenders in a disciplined system. Raw athleticism with positional versatility is developable. What must arrive: range, tackling ability, and football IQ.
S.scheme_fit_narrative: Safety is the most important defensive position in our scheme. We need chess pieces — players who can line up anywhere and do everything. The Big Nickel makes our Dime defense work. Without a versatile safety who can fit the run, cover the slot, and play deep, the whole system breaks down. Give us an athletic, smart, physical safety and we'll turn them into a star.

scheme_draft_intelligence:
The 2026 Ravens are undergoing the most significant coaching and schematic transition in franchise history — a new head coach (Minter), new OC (Doyle), new DC (Weaver), and wholesale staff turnover. The draft approach must reflect this transition in three critical ways.

First, the offensive line philosophy is shifting dramatically. Under Ledford, the run scheme is moving from inside zone/gap concepts to outside zone primary. This means the Ravens should be targeting athletic, lighter OL prospects — particularly at center (Linderbaum departed) and guard (Faalele departed). DeCosta explicitly acknowledged this shift at the Combine, noting the new staff may pursue 'different players' than in past years. IOL who test well in the short shuttle and can move laterally are now premium targets. The 2026 draft class's athletic center and guard prospects become Baltimore's top priority picks.

Second, the defensive scheme creates unique value at safety and EDGE. Minter's Dime-heavy, zone-based defense demands versatile hybrid safeties more than any other team in the NFL. A prospect like a large, athletic safety who can play in the box, slot, and deep field has disproportionate value to Baltimore because of how frequently Minter deploys six DBs. Similarly, EDGEs who can rush the passer AND set the edge in run defense have elevated value — Minter's 'earn the right to rush the passer' philosophy means pure speed rushers are devalued relative to other teams.

Third, the TE position represents hidden value. With Likely and Kolar both departing in free agency, the Ravens have a massive TE need behind Mark Andrews. Doyle's entire coaching background is TE development — he is the best TE-developing OC in the NFL based on his track record under Payton. A raw, athletic TE prospect with developmental upside is worth more to Baltimore than almost any other team because Doyle can develop the receiving skills while Ledford develops the blocking. This creates a draft inefficiency: TEs that other teams might pass on due to rawness become high-value picks for the Ravens.

Historically, the Ravens have been one of the NFL's best drafting organizations, and DeCosta has shown a willingness to adapt his approach to coaching philosophy shifts (as he did when Monken replaced Roman). Minter's 'personnel-driven' philosophy suggests the staff will identify their best players and build the scheme around them, rather than rigidly demanding specific archetypes. This means athletic upside and versatility are more valuable than ever — the scheme will adapt to what the players do best.
